benchmarks.name: Authentication
benchmarks.value: N/A (embedded)

sections.narrative: Access control is TrustDB's strongest differentiator, backed by 116 CBAC tests, 110 RDKC tests, 23 EACB tests, and 168 adversarial gym test functions. CBAC implements a 6-state governance circuit breaker (Open, Restricted, Suspended, Quarantined, Sealed, EngagementAuthorized) with compare-and-swap semantics. REWG derives entitlements from graph position via bitwise AND — no ACL tables, no policy engine. EACB provides FROST threshold signatures for LAWS 3000.09 human-in-the-loop authorization. Most critically, access control is cryptographic: RDKC derives unique per-page keys from governance state (verified by gym_c02 with 31 correctness tests including avalanche, determinism, and collision detection). A CUI-clearance entity cannot derive the key for a SECRET page — this is verified by gym_g01 clearance boundary battery (6 named attack scenarios) and gym_p01 pre-redaction leakage proof (7 memory-level tests). The gap to 9.5: FIPS ACVP formal validation, timing side-channel proof (gym_s01 exists with 7 tests but needs target-hardware validation), and ML-DSA-87 post-quantum signatures on CBAC audit entries.

history.note: CBAC 6-state (PRE-04), RDKC+CBAC wired (B01), EACB/FROST (B02), REWG wired (PRE-03). 116 CBAC tests, 110 RDKC tests, 168 adversarial gym functions. Up from 2.0.

benchmarks.name: WAL Tamper Protection
benchmarks.value: FNV-1a checksum (non-cryptographic) — HMAC-SHA384 upgrade pending (PRE-05)

sections.narrative: Encryption is TrustDB's defining capability, backed by 110 RDKC tests, 41 CAM tests, 10 page_crypto tests, 9 transport tests, 25 rekey tests, and 89 adversarial gym test functions across 10 gym files. Every 16KB page is encrypted with AES-256-GCM-SIV (nonce-misuse resistant) using a unique key derived by RDKC from four governance inputs: CBAC state, GEMVT clearance, REWG entitlements, and temporal window. This is verified by gym_e02 (10 input independence tests proving each governance dimension is a live cryptographic dependency) and gym_c02 (31 correctness tests: determinism across 10K runs, avalanche effect, 1M collision search). Structural key expiry is verified by gym_e01 (12 tests proving keys become mathematically underivable when governance state changes — not revoked, not expired, impossible to derive). Post-quantum key transport (ML-KEM-1024 + X25519 hybrid, 9 tests) exceeds every competitor. WAF=3.03 invariance proves the entire stack adds zero additional I/O. Two gaps to 9.5: FIPS 140-3 formal submission (ACVP vectors exist in gym_c01 but haven't been submitted to NIST) and WAL HMAC upgrade (PRE-05, currently FNV-1a checksum).

history.note: Full per-page encryption: RDKC (A01, 110 tests), AES-256-GCM-SIV (B01, 10 page_crypto tests), ML-KEM-1024 transport (B03, 9 tests), CNSA 2.0 profiles (A06, 41 CAM tests), re-encryption (A07, 25 tests), 89 adversarial gym functions. Gaps: FIPS formal submission, WAL HMAC. Up from 3.0.

benchmarks.name: WAL Integrity
benchmarks.value: FNV-1a checksum (non-cryptographic) — HMAC-SHA384 upgrade pending (PRE-05)

benchmarks.name: Temporal Log Export (Compliance)
benchmarks.value: Not implemented — no SOX/HIPAA/NIST formatted export

benchmarks.name: SIEM Integration
benchmarks.value: Not implemented — no CEF/STIX event export

sections.narrative: Auditing is strong on foundations but incomplete on output formats. ProvenanceChain tracks who/what/when on every entity at zero query overhead (inline with LeafEntry, WAF 3.03 invariant). TCI discovers implicit entity relationships at flush time — entirely novel, no competitor offers this. CBAC state transition logging records every governance change with CAS-versioned immutability (verified by 116 CBAC tests and 20 adversarial gym tests). The score is held at 7.0 by three gaps: (1) WAL uses FNV-1a checksum, not cryptographic HMAC — any attacker with disk access could tamper with audit entries undetected (PRE-05 upgrade is 3h of work and the single highest-leverage task for the entire security pillar), (2) no compliance-formatted export (SOX/HIPAA/NIST AU-10 requires structured temporal log export), (3) no SIEM integration (DoD monitoring infrastructure expects CEF/STIX formatted events). Fixing WAL HMAC alone would move this to 8.0. Adding compliance export and SIEM integration would reach 9.0+.

history.note: CBAC audit trail added. 48 adversarial gym functions verify audit integrity. Gaps: WAL HMAC (PRE-05), compliance export (G06), SIEM integration (H06).
